original_abstract:
We report the topotactic oxidative insertion of weakly coordinating anions into the redox-active metal–organic framework (MOF) Fe₂(dobpdc). While insertion reactions involving cations are well-documented in battery chemistry, the reversible accommodation of large anions like BF₄⁻ and PF₆⁻ is typically hindered by the rigidity of 3D host lattices. Here, we demonstrate that Fe₂(dobpdc) utilizes its expansive one-dimensional channels and dense chains of iron(II) centers to overcome these limitations. Chemical and electrochemical oxidation transform the material into a mixed-valence state, evidenced by the appearance of a broad intervalence charge-transfer band in the near-infrared region and a modest contraction of the unit cell. 

Characterization via ⁵⁷Fe Mössbauer spectroscopy and X-ray diffraction confirms that the framework maintains structural integrity through stoichiometric metal-based redox processes. Additionally, studies on the isostructural Mg-based analogue reveal that ligand-centered oxidation is also possible, though less reversible than the metal-based transition. The electrochemical performance of the MOF shows significant dependency on anion size and solvent environment, reflecting the unique dynamics of ion transport within nanoconfined pores. Leveraging these properties, we developed a prototype sodium dual-ion battery using Fe₂(dobpdc) as the cathode. The system exhibits a high initial reduction potential and achieves a maximum specific energy of 316 Wh/kg, maintaining stability over 50 cycles. This work highlights the potential of redox-active MOFs as high-energy density electrodes for advanced dual-ion storage systems.
humanized_abstract:
We present evidence for the topotactic oxidative insertion of weakly coordinating anions into the redox-active metal-organic framework (MOF) Fe₂(dobpdc). While insertion reactions of cations into MOFs are well documented in battery applications, the reversible incorporation of large anions such as BF₄⁻ and PF₆⁻ is often precluded by the structural rigidity of three-dimensional host frameworks. Instead, the expanded one-dimensional channels and networks of iron(II) in the structure of Fe₂(dobpdc) facilitate both chemical and electrochemical oxidation into a mixed valence state, marked by the appearance of a broad IVCT band in the near-IR and slight unit cell contraction.

The redox chemistry of Fe2(dobpdc) characterized using ⁵⁷Fe Mössbauer spectroscopy and X-ray diffraction shows that all stoichiometric redox processes occur through a framework that does not change over more than 150 cycles, indicating reversibility of the metal-centred processes. Studies on the isostructural Mg analogue demonstrate ligand-centred redox processes, which are, however, less reversible than the metal-centred ones. The electrochemical performance of the MOF depended strongly on the anion size as well as the nature of the solvent used for electrolyte preparation of the nanoconfined pores in MOFs leading to impressive sodium dual-ion battery prototype based on Fe₂(dobpdc) as the cathode material exhibiting an initial reduction potential up to 1 V vs. Na⁺/Na and discharge capability up to 316 Wh/kg and stable performance within 50 cycles. Redox active MOFs promise great potential as high energy density electrode materials for double-ion batteries.

document.output:
We present evidence for the topotactic oxidative insertion of weakly coordinating anions into the redox-active metal-organic framework (MOF) Fe₂(dobpdc). While insertion reactions of cations into MOFs are well documented in battery applications, the reversible incorporation of large anions such as BF₄⁻ and PF₆⁻ is often precluded by the structural rigidity of three-dimensional host frameworks. Instead, the expanded one-dimensional channels and networks of iron(II) in the structure of Fe₂(dobpdc) facilitate both chemical and electrochemical oxidation into a mixed valence state, marked by the appearance of a broad IVCT band in the near-IR and slight unit cell contraction.

The redox chemistry of Fe2(dobpdc) characterized using ⁵⁷Fe Mössbauer spectroscopy and X-ray diffraction shows that all stoichiometric redox processes occur through a framework that does not change over more than 150 cycles, indicating reversibility of the metal-centred processes. Studies on the isostructural Mg analogue demonstrate ligand-centred redox processes, which are, however, less reversible than the metal-centred ones. The electrochemical performance of the MOF depended strongly on the anion size as well as the nature of the solvent used for electrolyte preparation of the nanoconfined pores in MOFs leading to impressive sodium dual-ion battery prototype based on Fe₂(dobpdc) as the cathode material exhibiting an initial reduction potential up to 1 V vs. Na⁺/Na and discharge capability up to 316 Wh/kg and stable performance within 50 cycles. Redox active MOFs promise great potential as high energy density electrode materials for double-ion batteries.
document.input:
We report the topotactic oxidative insertion of weakly coordinating anions into the redox-active metal–organic framework (MOF) Fe₂(dobpdc). While insertion reactions involving cations are well-documented in battery chemistry, the reversible accommodation of large anions like BF₄⁻ and PF₆⁻ is typically hindered by the rigidity of 3D host lattices. Here, we demonstrate that Fe₂(dobpdc) utilizes its expansive one-dimensional channels and dense chains of iron(II) centers to overcome these limitations. Chemical and electrochemical oxidation transform the material into a mixed-valence state, evidenced by the appearance of a broad intervalence charge-transfer band in the near-infrared region and a modest contraction of the unit cell. 

Characterization via ⁵⁷Fe Mössbauer spectroscopy and X-ray diffraction confirms that the framework maintains structural integrity through stoichiometric metal-based redox processes. Additionally, studies on the isostructural Mg-based analogue reveal that ligand-centered oxidation is also possible, though less reversible than the metal-based transition. The electrochemical performance of the MOF shows significant dependency on anion size and solvent environment, reflecting the unique dynamics of ion transport within nanoconfined pores. Leveraging these properties, we developed a prototype sodium dual-ion battery using Fe₂(dobpdc) as the cathode. The system exhibits a high initial reduction potential and achieves a maximum specific energy of 316 Wh/kg, maintaining stability over 50 cycles. This work highlights the potential of redox-active MOFs as high-energy density electrodes for advanced dual-ion storage systems.
